core_content.markdown:
## Linear Independence and Dependence of Vectors 

Given any set of M vectors ${\pmb a}_{(1)},\cdots,{\pmb a}_{(m)}$ (with the same number r of components),a linear combination of these vectors S1s is an expression of the form 

$$c_{1}{\pmb a}_{(1)},+c_{2}{\pmb a}_{(2)},+\cdots+c_{m}{\pmb a}_{(m)}$$

where $c_{1},c_{2},\cdots,c_{m}$ are any scalars.

Now consider the equation 

(1)

$$c_{1}{\pmb a}_{(1)},+c_{2}{\pmb a}_{(2)},+\cdots+c_{m}{\pmb a}_{(m)}={\pmb0}equation (1) holds if we choose all $$

Clearly,this vector equation (1) holds if we choose all ${C_{j}}^{\prime}S~\mathcal{Z}\mathcal{Z}\mathcal{Z}\mathcal{O},$ because then 71becomes $\it{0}=0$ 



If this is the only m-tuple of scalars for which (1) holds,then Our vectors ${\pmb a}_{(1)},\cdots,{\pmb a}_{(m)}$ are s said to form a linearly independent t set $\mathbf{o r},$ more briefly,we call them linearly independent.
